data.title: OrthoPediatrics raises 2026 revenue outlook to $263M-$267M while targeting free cash flow breakeven
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OrthoPediatrics (KIDS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “2026 started strong with 13% first quarter revenue growth, further highlighted by significant improvements in adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow over prior year,” said (President, CEO & Director David Bailey), while noting “a softer start to the first quarter due to weather-related shutdowns in many of our OPSB clinics in January and February, but trends rebounded in March.”

  • “Importantly, we're at the earliest stages of a multiyear innovation super cycle,” (President, CEO & Director Bailey) said, citing “small contributions from recent beta launches, including 3P Hip and VerteGlide,” and adding, “Early trends are reinforcing our expectations for higher ASPs, margin expansion and improved capital efficiency as each of these products continue to scale.”

  • (President, CEO & Director Bailey) tied the strategy to 2026 targets: “we are raising our 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $263 million to $267 million… and reaffirming our expectations for approximately $25 million in adjusted EBITDA and full year free cash flow breakeven.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Fred Hite) summarized the quarter: “our first quarter of 2026 worldwide revenue of $59.4 million increased 13% compared to the first quarter of 2025,” and “Adjusted EBITDA was $2.2 million in the first quarter of 2026 compared to a loss of $0.4 million in the first quarter of 2025.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) highlighted a financing change: “On March 31, we amended our existing credit agreement… to add $20 million delayed draw term loan facility,” adding, “this structure allows us to preserve liquidity and avoid dilution… interest-only through maturity in 2029.”

Outlook

  • (President, CEO & Director Bailey) stated, “we are raising our 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $263 million to $267 million in revenue, representing 11% to 13% growth and reaffirming our expectations for approximately $25 million in adjusted EBITDA and full year free cash flow breakeven.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) added, “We also continue to expect to generate approximately $25 million of adjusted EBITDA, deploy approximately $10 million in [sets] and to achieve free cash flow breakeven in 2026,” and cautioned that “some periods of free cash flow will be negative and others positive.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management lifted the 2026 revenue range from $262 million-$266 million (Q4 call) to $263 million-$267 million (Q1 call), while keeping the adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow breakeven framing unchanged.

Financial Results

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) reported segment revenue detail: “Trauma and Deformity global revenue of $43.0 million… increased 14%,” “scoliosis global revenue of $15.4 million increased 13%,” and “Sports Medicine other revenue… was $0.9 million, which stayed consistent year-over-year.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) said “gross profit margin was 73%,” while “Total operating expense increased… to $51.7 million,” including “Sales and marketing… to $18.5 million” and “General and administrative… to $31.0 million.”

  • (CFO, Principal Financial & Accounting Officer, COO & Director Hite) said “GAAP net loss per share… was $0.45” and “Non-GAAP net loss per share… was $0.42,” and added liquidity and cash-use items: “We ended the first quarter with $50.9 million in cash, short-term investments and restricted cash,” “Set deployment was $2.3 million,” and “Free cash flow used… was $5.0 million.”

Q&A

  • Mathew Blackman, TD Cowen: asked about quantifying weather disruption and whether it was recaptured; (CFO Hite) replied, “typically, those appointments get rescheduled,” and said “the vast majority of those got cleared in the month of March…,” while confirming some “soot into the second quarter.”

  • Frederick Wise, Stifel: asked why guidance wasn’t moved more after strong execution; (CFO Hite) responded, “we like to wait for those things to show up and to show up in the numbers before we get too far ahead of ourselves,” and added, “we'll continue to stay conservative.”

  • [Izzy], on for Ryan Zimmerman, BTIG: asked if 22% international growth is a baseline; (President Bailey) said, “we're not going to get too far ahead of ourselves on 22% growth,” and added that EU MDR set timing is “a bit of the rate limiter, certainly not demand,” while also saying, “we do expect international to outgrow the domestic market for each quarter for the rest of the year.”

  • Joseph, on for Mike Matson, Needham: asked about operating leverage and gross margin; (CFO Hite) said, “the true leverage down to the EBITDA line is coming from G&A,” and “the leverage we saw in the first quarter will be similar in the rest of the year.”

  • David Turkaly, Citizens JMP: asked on Veraxis timing/clearance; (CFO Hite) said, “we expect first surgeries… by the end of the year,” and “it is a 510(k)… not yet cleared, but we would expect it in the back half of the year.”

  • Ravi Misra, Truist: pressed on balancing growth vs. set deployment; (President Bailey) answered, “there's certainly a healthy tension… to accelerate revenue versus generate positive free cash flow,” and said, “Our commitment is unwavering… to… free cash flow breakeven… in 2026.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with supportive framing but repeated probing on guidance conservatism, international sustainability, and whether set deployment is constraining growth (e.g., Wise: “Why leave guidance… unchanged?” and Misra: “why don't… accelerate the set deployment to capture revenue”).

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and measured in Q&A, emphasizing demand and new-product cadence while reinforcing caution on timing (e.g., (CFO Hite): “stay conservative,” and (President Bailey): “we're not going to get too far ahead of ourselves”).

  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1’s tone leaned more execution-and-timing focused (weather, set availability, 510(k) timing), while Q4 leaned more on milestone messaging (including “our first quarter of positive free cash flow in the company's history”).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from reiteration in Q4 (“We are reiterating our 2026 revenue guidance of $262 million to $266 million”) to an increase in Q1 (“raising our 2026 revenue guidance… to… $263 million to $267 million”), while keeping “approximately $25 million” adjusted EBITDA and “free cash flow breakeven” intact.

  • Product cycle framing remained consistent (both calls used “multiyear… super cycle”), but Q1 added more near-term commercialization detail, including (President Bailey) on VerteGlide: “This success is triggering our move to full market release… in the second quarter.”

  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s shareholder-value optionality and new adjacencies to Q1’s operational pacing questions (set availability, ramp timing, and conservative guidance posture).

Risks and Concerns

  • (President Bailey) flagged operational disruption risk from weather impacts on clinics, describing “weather-related shutdowns in many of our OPSB clinics in January and February.”

  • (President Bailey) and (CFO Hite) repeatedly described timing constraints around inventory and approvals, including Europe where set timing was described as “the rate limiter,” and Veraxis where clearance timing was called “a bit of a wildcard.”

  • (CFO Hite) indicated free cash flow will fluctuate intra-year: “some periods of free cash flow will be negative and others positive,” even as the company targets full-year breakeven.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a rebound quarter after early weather disruption, driven by international strength and continued 20%+ OPSB growth, while positioning 3P Hip and VerteGlide as early contributors within a “multiyear innovation super cycle.” The company raised its 2026 revenue range to $263 million-$267 million and reaffirmed approximately $25 million of adjusted EBITDA and full-year free cash flow breakeven, while emphasizing that set availability, EU MDR rollout logistics, and 510(k) timing will influence how quickly new products translate into reported results.

data.title: ConocoPhillips outlines $12B-$12.5B 2026 capex range while staying on track for $7B free cash flow inflection by 2029
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ConocoPhillips (COP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We generated $2.4 billion of free cash flow and returned $2 billion of capital to our shareholders," said (Chairman & CEO Ryan Lance), while framing the quarter against Middle East-driven market disruption and emphasizing that "we will continue delivering base dividend growth competitive with the top quartile of the S&P 500" and "remain committed to returning 45% of our CFO."
  • "In Alaska, we're winding down another successful winter construction season with the Willow project now 50% complete," said (Chairman & CEO Ryan Lance), adding that ConocoPhillips "recently completed our 4-well exploration program in Alaska" and "Port Arthur LNG project continues to progress very well with first LNG expected next year."
  • "We generated $1.89 per share in adjusted earnings and $5.4 billion of CFO," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO Andrew O'Brien), adding "Capital expenditures were $2.9 billion" and that the company "returned $2 billion to our shareholders during the first quarter, $1 billion in ordinary dividends and $1 billion of share repurchases."
  • "For production, the midpoint of our annual guidance is updated at 2,310,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO Andrew O'Brien), while stressing, "To be clear, this is not a call on when we think the conflict will resolve. We're simply trying to provide a clear and transparent framework for you to model."

Outlook

  • "The midpoint of our second quarter production guidance is 2,200,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), attributing the change to "the full exclusion of Qatar production from guidance for the quarter, the Surmont royalty rate adjustment and planned second quarter maintenance."
  • "For capital spending, we're updating our guidance to a range of $12 billion to $12.5 billion versus our prior guidance of about $12 billion," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), citing "slightly more Permian activity over the second half of the year" and added uncertainty tied to "timing for NFE and NFS spending."
  • "Full year guidance of $10.2 billion is unchanged," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), while stating it reflects "a $400 million reduction from 2025" and that management "remain[s] confident in realizing the full $1 billion run rate by year-end."

Financial Results

  • "We produced 2,309,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO Andrew O'Brien), noting it "includes the impacts of the Middle East conflict on Qatar volumes and higher royalty rates at Surmont from higher oil prices" and adding that Lower 48 output was "1,453,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day."
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and short-term investments of $6.7 billion as well as $1.2 billion in liquid long-term investments," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien).
  • "We remain unhedged on oil and LNG to ensure we capture the price upside," said (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), adding that "shareholders are directly participating in this upside as we remain committed to returning 45% of our CFO."

Q&A

  • Scott Hanold, RBC Capital Markets: asked for COP’s view of physical vs. financial oil market dynamics and likely operator reactions; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said "for about 2 months now, we've had about 10 million barrels a day of production offline" and that the shortfall is being absorbed by "refinery run cuts and demand curtailments," while (Chairman & CEO Lance) said "the floor probably is going to have to raise up" relative to the prior mid-cycle view.
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: asked for a Willow mark-to-market and key milestones; (Executive Vice President of Global Operations & Technical Functions Kirk Johnson) said Willow is "50% complete," the team completed "the entirety of the gravel scope," and "this is on track" for "early oil expectation ... for 2029."
  • Wei Jiang, Barclays: asked why COP leaned into Permian activity and what would drive further flex; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said the $250 million midpoint CapEx increase is about maintaining "steady state" and responding to partner ballots, while (Executive Vice President of Lower 48 & Global HSE Nicholas Olds) said "we're adding another Permian rig" and "we're not going to elect out of low cost of supply" nonoperated wells.
  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: questioned sticking with the 45% CFO payout amid higher prices and buybacks; (Chairman & CEO Lance) said the company buys back shares "on a dollar cost average" and "we do want to make sure we hit the 45% made up between the base dividend and whatever shares we're repurchasing."
  • Francis Lloyd Byrne, Jefferies: asked why OpEx guidance wasn’t lowered after a strong Q1; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said, "it is only the first quarter" and management wants "a little bit more time" before changing guidance.
  • Devin McDermott, Morgan Stanley: asked about Equatorial Guinea (EG) tolling and Port Arthur marketing; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said COP has "already placed the first 5 million tons" of Port Arthur Phase 1 and discussions are "intensifying," while (Executive Vice President Johnson) said the EG tolling agreement "pushes the life of that asset ... well into the 2030s."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: asked how Middle East disruptions affect LNG macro and Qatar project timing; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said Qatar disruption equates to "200 cargoes" not delivered and COP sees a "structural change" toward shortages, while (Executive Vice President Johnson) said QE expects NFE/NFS delays "to the tune of months."
  • Joshua Silverstein, UBS: asked about divestitures and Port Arthur Phase 1 stake; (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said the $5 billion program has "$3 billion ... already behind us" and COP is "not going to be schedule driven," adding, "we certainly don't need to sell anything" at Port Arthur while it is under construction.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing around macro volatility, capital returns, and timing risk, including questions on whether to change payouts and how to model conflict impacts.
  • Management tone was neutral to slightly positive, repeatedly emphasizing framework and durability, including "To be clear, this is not a call on when we think the conflict will resolve" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) and "these are no-brainers" on Permian continuity (Chairman & CEO Lance).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the current call’s tone was more conflict-driven and uncertainty-focused, while Q4 emphasized execution and multi-year free cash flow trajectory without the same level of geopolitical disruption discussion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 updated 2026 capital guidance to "$12 billion to $12.5 billion" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) versus the prior quarter’s "2026 capital spend guidance of about $12 billion" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien).
  • Q1 guidance language added explicit conflict modeling, including Qatar being "excluded from second quarter production guidance" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien), while Q4 guidance centered on cost and capital reductions and "modest growth for the year" (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien).
  • Analyst focus shifted from organic vs. M&A posture, reserves, and breakeven trajectory in Q4 to near-term operational and financial implications of Middle East supply disruptions, Qatar LNG timing, and short-cycle U.S. activity response in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The macro environment remains volatile and pretty impossible to predict," said (Chairman & CEO Ryan Lance), while (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) said guidance updates reflect "the uncertainty surrounding the Middle East conflict" and included a range around NFE/NFS spending timing.
  • (Executive VP of Strategy & Commercial and CFO O'Brien) flagged that the supply shortfall is being absorbed by "refinery run cuts and demand curtailments" and said COP is "downgrading our view of global oil demand to be flat year-over-year" if the conflict persists.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter with $2.4 billion of free cash flow and $2 billion returned to shareholders, while updating 2026 guidance to reflect Middle East uncertainty, including Qatar’s exclusion from Q2 production guidance and a higher capital range of $12 billion to $12.5 billion tied to modest Permian additions and LNG project timing variability; leadership reiterated its 45% CFO return objective, highlighted Willow at 50% complete with early oil expected in 2029, and pointed to LNG positioning in Equatorial Guinea and Port Arthur as central to its longer-term free cash flow growth plan.

data.title: Imax outlines 2026 targets including $1.4B global box office and 160-175 system installations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IMAX Corporation (IMAX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "With the heart of our formidable slate now rolling out, we remain very confident in our 2026 guidance, including a record $1.4 billion in global box office this year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "Project Hail Mary... has now earned more than $90 million in IMAX, more than double our initial projections." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "IMAX global box office in the current quarter-to-date is over $100 million, up over 10% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "Year-to-date, we signed agreements for over 40 new and upgraded IMAX systems worldwide... most recently with our biggest deal ever in one of the most productive markets in the world, our 10-system agreement with HOYTS in Australia and New Zealand." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "I'm happy to share that I'm making excellent progress, and I'm gradually resuming oversight of the business and involved in all key strategic decisions." (CEO & Director Richard L. Gelfond)

Outlook

  • "We remain very well positioned to achieve our 2026 guidance, including record global box office of $1.4 billion, 160 to 175 system installations worldwide and adjusted EBITDA margin... with at least 45%." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "We remain confident in our forecast of total adjusted EBITDA margin of more than 50% in the coming year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "[T]here's always a mix between the regions of box office... and the amount that we're investing into marketing in this year... And so that's where the margins can ebb and flow." (Executive VP & CFO Fernandes)

Financial results

  • "First quarter adjusted net income grew 33% to $10 million, while adjusted EPS grew $0.17, up $0.04 year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "IMAX delivered revenues of $81.4 million, a decline of $5 million year-over-year, driven by decreased revenues in Greater China." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "Adjusted EBITDA declined in line with revenue, down $6 million year-over-year to $31 million... adjusted EBITDA margin was 38% compared to 43% in the prior year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "In our Content Solutions segment, revenues declined 8% to $31 million... [and] China box office declined 62% due to an exceptionally strong Q1 in the prior year and the timing of major releases this year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "IMAX cash flow from operations was $4 million compared to $7 million in the prior year and includes $8 million in higher year-over-year lease incentives provided to exhibitors to support the building of new IMAX auditoriums." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • "As of March 31, we held $146 million in cash and $300 million in debt with a net leverage of 0.86x." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)

Q&A

  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "[D]oes that assume a global box office of $1.4 billion?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[Y]es... there's always a mix between the regions of box office... and the amount that we're investing into marketing in this year... this is just from a guidance perspective, providing that guidance with respect to the floor of the 45%."
  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "[A]ny disruptions created by the U.S.-Iran conflict...?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "No, not at all... we have about 35 locations within the region... majority are continuing to operate."
  • Michael Hickey, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: "[F]rame... the growth opportunity network-wise in Australia and Japan?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "Australia is one of the strongest performing regions... Some of the locations have TSAs up to $4 million... We're only about 13% penetrated... Japan is only 47% penetrated."
  • Eric Handler, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC, Research Division: "$8 million of higher lease incentives, is that part of that strategy?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "It is... Investing the $8 million to help grow the network faster... all within the respect of making sure that we hit our return hurdles."
  • Chad Beynon, Macquarie Research: "[W]hat gives you the confidence that China will come through in '26?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[W]e're managing it on an annual global portfolio... there's a lot of Hollywood titles that have strong potential in China... [and] the local language slate is stacking up as well."
  • David Karnovsky, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "[A] read-through... to kind of your relationship with the studio?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[W]e feel it's a pure marketing play... Marvel fans, we believe that they're among the savviest... moviegoers out there."
  • Steven Frankel, Rosenblatt Securities Inc., Research Division: "Where are we in that process today?" Executive VP & CFO Fernandes: "[W]e're only about 28% to 30% penetrated in India... it does take long to install... Ramayana Part 1... [is] a really big deal because over 90% of their box office is local language."

Sentiment analysis

  • "[A]djusted EBITDA margin guidance with a floor of 45%" and China/local-language pacing were recurring analyst focus areas, with questions framed as clarification-seeking rather than confrontational. (Analyst sentiment: neutral)
  • "We remain very confident in our 2026 guidance" and "There has never been a better time to be in the IMAX business" anchored prepared remarks, while Q&A included more qualifying language such as "margin will fluctuate" and "it's a mix" when discussing profitability cadence and China timing. (Management sentiment: slightly positive)
  • In Q4 2025, management used more record-year emphasis ("record-breaking year," "We beat projections across almost every key financial metrics"), while Q1 2026 messaging leaned more to reaffirmation of full-year targets and explaining geographic mix and marketing spend. (Tone shift vs. prior quarter: from celebratory to execution-focused)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 leadership presence shifted, with "Natasha will handle today's call and Q&A session" and CEO Richard L. Gelfond noting he is on a "Leave of Absence" and "gradually resuming oversight," versus Rich Gelfond leading prepared remarks and Q&A heavily in Q4 2025.
  • The 2026 framework was reiterated in Q1 2026 ("$1.4 billion" global box office; "160 to 175" installations; EBITDA margin floor "45%"), consistent with Q4 2025 guidance language ("projected $1.4 billion"; "160 to 175"; "floor of 45%").
  • China discussion in Q1 2026 emphasized diversification and timing normalization ("more evenly spread throughout the year") versus Q4 2025 framing that Q1 would be the lowest quarter and China would be more balanced due to title timing.

Risks and concerns

  • "[W]e faced a significant comp against last year's massive Ne Zha 2" and "China box office declined 62%" were cited as near-term pressures, with mitigation framed as timing and mix: "We expect IMAX box office in China to be more evenly spread throughout the year." (Executive VP & CFO Natasha Fernandes)
  • Marketing spend and margin variability were highlighted as factors affecting EBITDA cadence: "we are marketing titles well in advance" and "margin will fluctuate normally quarter-by-quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Fernandes)
  • On geopolitics, management said: "No, not at all" regarding U.S.-Iran conflict impact on annual outlook, while noting the Middle East footprint and continued operations. (Executive VP & CFO Fernandes)

Final takeaway

IMAX management reiterated full-year 2026 targets (including $1.4 billion global box office, 160 to 175 system installations, and an adjusted EBITDA margin floor of 45%) while attributing Q1 year-over-year pressure primarily to Greater China comps and timing. Management emphasized early Q2 box office momentum, a broadened and higher-visibility slate across Hollywood and local language, and continued network expansion—highlighted by a 10-system HOYTS agreement—alongside disciplined operating expenses and selective capital deployment (including lease incentives) aimed at accelerating new IMAX locations ahead of major releases.

data.title: SunCoke targets $230M-$250M 2026 adjusted EBITDA as Middletown power resumes late Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SunCoke Energy (SXC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're pleased with our performance in the first quarter, delivering consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $56.5 million, reflecting strong operational execution," said (CEO, President & Director Katherine Gates), while noting operational disruption: "As discussed on our fourth quarter 2025 earnings call, our coke plants were impacted by severe winter weather and the Middletown turbine failure."

  • (CEO, President & Director Katherine Gates) framed capital returns and balance sheet priorities around cash generation: "Earlier today, we also announced a quarterly dividend of $0.12 per share... This is our 27th consecutive quarter announcing a dividend," and "We had strong operating cash flow generation of $72.7 million and ended the quarter with ample liquidity of $262 million."

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) summarized profitability and the key drivers: "Net loss attributable to SunCoke was $0.05 per share in the first quarter of 2026," and "The decrease was primarily driven by higher depreciation expense, the shutdown of our Haverhill 1 cokemaking facility, severe winter weather and the lower power sales due to Middletown turbine failure."

  • (CEO, President & Director Katherine Gates) emphasized contracted positioning and integration execution: "As previously discussed, we are running at full capacity and sold out for the full year," and linked that to operating catalysts: "With the continued seamless integration of Phoenix, the resumption of power production at Middletown and continued strong operational execution, we are confident we will achieve full year 2026 consolidated adjusted EBITDA within our guidance range of $230 million to $250 million."

Outlook

  • (CEO, President & Director Katherine Gates) reiterated full-year consolidated profitability expectations: "We are confident that we'll be able to deliver full year consolidated adjusted EBITDA within our guidance range of $230 million to $250 million."

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) reaffirmed segment guideposts while describing Q1 operational normalization: "We are reaffirming our full year domestic coke adjusted EBITDA guidance of $162 million to $168 million," and "We expect our Industrial Services segment to continue delivering strong results throughout the balance of the year and are reaffirming our full year 2026 Industrial Services adjusted EBITDA guidance range of $90 million to $100."

  • On the cadence of recovery, (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) said: "We are expecting power production to resume at Middletown late in the second quarter," and added on operations, "We are confident we'll make up the lost production from the first quarter during the balance of the year."

Financial Results

  • (CEO, President & Director Katherine Gates) highlighted cash generation and liquidity: "We had strong operating cash flow generation of $72.7 million and ended the quarter with ample liquidity of $262 million."

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) detailed the quarter’s EBITDA and segment mix: "Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of 2026 was $56.5 million," while "First quarter domestic coke adjusted EBITDA was $35.3 million and coke sales volumes were 842,000 tons" and "Our Industrial Services segment generated $26.2 million of adjusted EBITDA."

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) described working-capital and capital allocation movements: "We generated strong operating cash flow of $72.7 million during the quarter, mainly driven by a reduction in coal and coke inventory and used $26 million for debt paydown," alongside "We spent $17 million on CapEx and paid $10.7 million in dividends at the rate of $0.12 per share this quarter."

Q&A

  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: "What was the main driver or drivers" of lower Domestic Coke EBITDA/ton and how to bridge to full-year? (CFO Agrawal): "the two main factors... is the winter weather impact to our operations and the Middletown turbine impact," adding, "the Middletown turbine is expected to be back in late Q2."

  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: "Is it fair" that the Q2 Middletown headwind could be "roughly... $5 million"? (CFO Agrawal): "That's kind of in the ballpark, yes."

  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked about Industrial EBITDA rising despite flat/down revenues and whether Phoenix/terminals efficiencies are driving it. (CFO Agrawal): "we are seeing significant improvement in the volumes that we are handling at terminals," and on Phoenix, "as we go through the remainder of the 2026, we expect to see some more of those synergies come through," while noting "some drag cost" from systems integration.

  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked whether higher SG&A was tied to integration/bonuses and if it repeats. (CFO Agrawal): "the accrual for the bonuses are different for '25 versus '26 given the performance of the company," and "Q1 2026 should be the run rate for the rest of the year."

  • Henry Hearle, B. Riley Securities: asked whether terminals could benefit from "Section 303 DPA" and potential DoD funding. (CEO Gates): "I don't think that those are going to be drivers to additional throughput necessarily," adding terminal guidance is "driven by demand primarily internationally for coal."

  • Henry Hearle, B. Riley Securities: asked for the share of CMT volumes that are thermal export. (CFO Agrawal): "we are not kind of breaking out" those items, and "if you go back and look at historical data where we used to break out, the ratio should remain the same."

  • Henry Hearle, B. Riley Securities: asked if Middle East conflict is increasing export thermal tons. (CEO Gates): "We are seeing certainly some higher pricing in the market, and that is leading to higher demand," and "there's definitely sort of a flow-through from that conflict."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to pressing on near-term cadence and transparency, including SG&A persistence and terminal mix, with questions such as "how should we think about SG&A kind of going forward?" and requests to quantify terminal exposure (CMT thermal export share).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, using confidence language such as (CEO Gates) "we are confident we will achieve full year 2026 consolidated adjusted EBITDA" and (CFO Agrawal) describing recovery timing: "back in late Q2" and "make that back up in Q3 and Q4."

  • Versus last quarter, prepared remarks shifted away from major one-time items and leadership transition language and toward execution and cadence; Q4 2025 emphasized "onetime items" and CFO transition, including (CEO Gates) "I would also like to congratulate Shantanu Agrawal on his well-deserved appointment as Chief Financial Officer," while Q1 2026 centered on weather/turbine impacts and reaffirmed guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language stayed consistent quarter to quarter on the headline range, with Q1 2026 reaffirming (CEO Gates) "$230 million to $250 million" and Q4 2025 guiding (Senior VP & CFO Mark Marinko) "between $230 million and $250 million in 2026."

  • Segment narrative evolved from Q4’s integration and market-recovery setup to Q1’s execution-and-recovery cadence: Q4 2025 said 2026 would "benefit from a full year of Phoenix Global" and "improved market conditions for our terminals," while Q1 2026 pointed to "seamless integration of Phoenix" and "sequential improvement in terminals handling volumes."

  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s Algoma/legal and Haverhill I shutdown context to Q1’s EBITDA/ton cadence, SG&A run-rate, and terminal export exposure; Q4 included "current status of litigation" and Haverhill I restart economics, while Q1 centered on "winter weather" and "Middletown turbine" bridging and repeatable costs.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) attributed Q1 pressure to operations and downtime, citing "severe winter weather" and "lower power sales due to Middletown turbine failure" and noting the turbine impact continuing into Q2 as "you will see that impact through majority of Q2 with no power production there."

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) flagged terminal results sensitivity to mix: Industrial Services gains were "partially offset by a change in mix of products handled at the terminals," while (CEO Gates) reiterated terminal throughput expectations are "driven by demand primarily internationally for coal."

  • (CFO & Treasurer Shantanu Agrawal) indicated higher ongoing corporate cost run-rate tied to performance-based compensation: "Q1 2026 should be the run rate for the rest of the year" for SG&A due to bonus accrual differences.

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 as a weather- and turbine-impacted quarter with recovery built into the remainder of the year, reiterating confidence in full-year 2026 consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $230 million to $250 million, supported by sold-out coke capacity, improving terminal volumes, Phoenix integration, and a late-Q2 resumption of Middletown power production. The call also reinforced capital allocation priorities around the $0.12 quarterly dividend, debt paydown, and a stated goal of gross leverage below 3x by the end of 2026.

data.title: Allegiant forecasts Q2 operating margin of 1% amid $4.35/gal fuel and plans 6.5% ASM cut
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Allegiant Travel Company (ALGT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We started the year on a very strong note," said (CEO & Director Gregory Anderson), citing "a 14.9% adjusted operating margin" and adding, "we achieved our highest first quarter adjusted operating margin since pre-COVID."

  • (CEO & Director Anderson) tied performance to reliability and capacity discipline: "Our operational performance was outstanding with a 99.9% controllable completion factor" and "we prioritize flexible capacity to capitalize on peak demand periods rather than chasing maximum utilization over the entire year."

  • (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Drew Wells) reported record top-line and unit revenue: "We finished the first quarter with $732.4 million in total revenue" and "producing a 1Q TRASM of $0.1431, up 16.4% year-over-year," adding that "both total revenue and TRASM represent first quarter records for the company."

  • (CEO & Director Anderson) highlighted revenue diversification and product traction: "Our co-branded credit card currently has over 600,000 cardholders" and "card remuneration represents just over 5% of our annual revenue," while adding, "Our premium seating product, Allegiant Extra, is continuing to outpace expectations."

  • "For the first quarter, we generated net income of $69.6 million, resulting in earnings per share of $3.77," said (President & CFO Robert Neal), while also stating, "Fuel averaged $3.04 per gallon in the quarter compared to our initial guide of $2.60."

  • On the Sun Country acquisition timeline, (President & CFO Neal) said, "we received DOT approval in April with the remaining step being shareholder votes... scheduled for May 8" and "the transaction should close around May 13."

Outlook

  • (CEO & Director Anderson) framed the main variable as fuel: "The main pressure point is jet fuel costs, which have risen sharply," and said the company is responding by "reducing off-peak capacity where margin pressure is most acute" and "reduced service on some of our longer stage length routes where the hurdle on fuel cost is higher."

  • (CEO & Director Anderson) updated Q2 capacity planning: "we are now planning for a 6.5% year-over-year reduction in ASMs in the second quarter, down from our initial plan at the start of the year," while adding, "we are not seeing any reasons to pull back on our peak flying."

  • (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) set expectations for unit revenue without giving a specific TRASM forecast: "we do expect second quarter year-over-year unit revenue growth to exceed the 16.4% delivered in the first quarter," and added, "we now expect second quarter capacity to be slightly lower than implied on the last call."

  • (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) also shifted the tone for 3Q planning versus prior expectations: "While we had previously anticipated modest growth in 3Q, we now expect capacity to be flat to perhaps slightly down year-over-year."

  • Analysts-estimates comparisons were not included because the provided estimates tag uses a numeric quarter format (e.g., fiscal_quarter: 1) rather than the required Q1–Q4 format.

Financial Results

  • (President & CFO Neal) said quarterly profitability was supported by margin initiatives and demand: "we delivered an adjusted operating margin of 14.9% and generated $168 million in EBITDA, resulting in an EBITDA margin of 22.9%."

  • (President & CFO Neal) described the cost setup and efficiency trend: "First quarter nonfuel unit costs were $0.0864, up 7.1% year-over-year, primarily driven by a 5.9% reduction in capacity," and "ASMs per gallon increase 1.2% year-over-year to 86.7."

  • Liquidity and leverage were emphasized as a strategic asset ahead of the merger and in a higher-fuel environment: "We ended the quarter... with total available liquidity of $1.2 billion," said (President & CFO Neal), adding, "we made $29.4 million of debt principal payments and ended the period with net leverage of 1.8x."

  • Capital spending and fleet updates were tied to ownership flexibility: "During the first quarter, we invested $176 million in capital expenditures," said (President & CFO Neal), and "we ended the quarter with 123 aircraft in operation, taking delivery of 1 737 MAX and retiring 1 A320."

Q&A

  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank AG: Asked whether June-quarter and potential 3Q capacity pullbacks were driven by fuel or the faster Sun Country closing; (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) responded, "Zero impact from the Sun Country time line or integration. This is purely a fuel-related decision."

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: Asked about fixed-fee flying as a lever in high fuel and the card program runway; (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) said, "I don't foresee any difference in aiming to in high fuel environments, focus on the lines of revenue that have the fuel pass-through," and on card remuneration added, "10% of revenue kind of being that stretch goal... I feel more confident today... saying that 10% is achievable."

  • Atul Maheswari, UBS: Asked if 2Q is likely the TRASM growth high watermark and what drives TRASM (yield vs. load); (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) answered, "I have to imagine the second quarter will be the high watermark," and added, "I expect to see some continued load factor expansion... yields will probably lead the way."

  • Carter Eades, Raymond James: Asked about cost implications of capacity changes and fuel recapture approach; (President & CFO Neal) said "unit costs for the year being up mid-single digits" remained "still achievable," while (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) said, "we didn't provide that explicitly" and described a mix of "refining and honing in capacity in the off-peaks" and "pushing fare where appropriate in the peaks."

  • John Godyn, Citigroup: Asked about MAX contribution and whether older A320 retirements could be accelerated; (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "This year... a little over 20% of our ASMs to be produced by the MAX aircraft... By 2028, we're going to get to about 50% of our ASMs," and (President & CFO Neal) added, "there is an opportunity to potentially accelerate some retirement of some of our older A320s, but we're not making any calls quite yet."

  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Asked how fuel changes affect synergy timing/magnitude and whether Q2 guidance includes Sun Country; (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "we still remain or retain a very high degree of conviction on... the $140 million," and (President & CFO Neal) clarified, "the guide is stand-alone Allegiant for the second quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on fuel-driven profitability and the mechanics of guidance and spreads, including Conor Cunningham’s, "I'm just trying to understand that a little bit better," and Scott Group’s focus on whether "CASM ex accelerates a little bit more in Q2 than TRASM."

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious/defensive on near-term profits, with (President & CFO Neal) stating, "we don't believe it would be valuable to provide updates to our full year guidance at the moment," while maintaining confidence on the deal: (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "we still remain... conviction" in "the $140 million" synergy target.

  • Versus last quarter, the tone shifted from profitability expansion and full-year EPS guidance to fuel volatility and near-term margin compression; in Q4, (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "For the full year, we're guiding to adjusted EPS of more than $8 per share," while in Q1, (President & CFO Neal) emphasized Q2 fuel-driven pressure.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture changed from a full-year EPS framework in Q4 to withholding full-year updates in Q1; (CEO & Director Anderson) previously said, "we're guiding to adjusted EPS of more than $8 per share," while (President & CFO Neal) now said, "we don't believe it would be valuable to provide updates to our full year guidance at the moment."

  • Capacity messaging pivoted from earlier 2026 expectations of an only slight annual decline to explicit cuts in the near term; in Q4, (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) said, "we would expect the second quarter to be the high point" for CASM-X and outlined a full-year capacity expectation, while in Q1, (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "planning for a 6.5% year-over-year reduction in ASMs in the second quarter" and (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) said 3Q is now expected "flat to perhaps slightly down."

  • Sun Country timing accelerated meaningfully; in Q4, (President & CFO Neal) said the deal was expected "to close in the back half of 2026," while in Q1 he said it "should close around May 13."

Risks and Concerns

  • Fuel volatility was described as the key near-term earnings risk; (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "crack spreads nearly tripled" before easing, and (President & CFO Neal) tied Q2 pressure to "an assumed fuel price of $4.35 per gallon" that is "driving nearly $120 million of incremental operating expense relative to expectations at the time of our last call."

  • Capacity reductions and off-peak exposure were positioned as mitigation; (CEO & Director Anderson) said, "we are navigating the volatility by reducing off-peak capacity" and (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Officer Wells) said he was "fine to maybe take a little less risk" by canceling earlier for passenger convenience.

  • Transaction-related uncertainty centered on timing and post-close reporting; (President & CFO Neal) said the company is "still working through how we expect to report and guide what segments will be" for the combined entity.

Final Takeaway

Allegiant’s Q1 results were framed by management as record revenue and strong adjusted margins driven by peak-demand scheduling, high controllable completion, and continued progress in co-brand and premium seating. For Q2, management shifted focus to fuel-driven downside, cutting capacity—primarily off-peak—and guiding to an operating margin of 1% and a (-$0.50) loss per share on a $4.35/gal fuel assumption. The call also moved the Sun Country timeline forward to a targeted close around May 13, with management reiterating confidence in $140 million of synergies while noting near-term guidance will remain stand-alone until post-close visibility improves.

data.title: Axos projects low- to mid-teens loan growth as $2.3B Jenius and $3.2B Capital One deposit deals advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Axos Financial, Inc. (AX) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “We generated another quarter of double-digit year-over-year growth in net interest income, ending loan and deposit balances, earnings per share and book value,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants).

  • “We generated almost $700 million in net loan growth linked quarter,” and “ending loan balances grew by approximately $800 million linked quarter, excluding single-family warehouse,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants), alongside “net interest margin was 4.57% for the quarter ended March 31, 2026, compared to 4.94% in the prior quarter.”

  • “Other highlights in the quarter include: noninterest income was $86 million…up from $53 million in the prior quarter,” and “excluding the benefit of the $22 million legal settlement this quarter, noninterest income was up approximately $10 million linked quarter,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants).

  • “We announced the acquisition of approximately $2.3 billion of online saving deposits from Jenius Bank in February of 2026,” and “last week, we announced a separate deposit acquisition of approximately $3.2 billion of IRA savings and CDs from Capital One,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants), adding, “we received regulatory approval last month and expect to complete the deposit conversion and client onboarding next month,” while the Capital One deal was described as targeting “a conversion and close in the second half of calendar 2026.”

  • “Provision for credit losses was $41 million in Q3 '26 compared to $25 million in Q2 '26,” driven by “a specific reserve of approximately $20 million for C&I loan,” said (Executive VP & CFO Derrick Walsh).

Outlook

  • “Taking all these factors into consideration, we are confident that we will generate loan growth by the low -- in the low to mid-teens on an annual basis this year,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants).

  • “We expect reported net interest margin to stay roughly flat on an organic basis, excluding the impact of the deposit purchase premium from the acquired deposits, which we estimate to be around 5 basis points,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants).

  • “Our expectation is to maintain an annual tax rate of approximately 26% to 27%, excluding these potential benefits,” said (Executive VP & CFO Derrick Walsh).

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s commentary shifted from describing a guided March-quarter NIM decline (previously tied to rate-cut betas and day count) to describing organic NIM as “roughly flat” while isolating the acquired-deposit premium amortization.

Financial Results

  • “Net income was approximately $124.7 million in the quarter ended March 31,” and “diluted EPS was $2.15,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants).

  • “Net interest margin was 4.57%…compared to 4.94% in the prior quarter,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants), who also noted the FDIC-purchased loan accretion benefit had “dwindled to around 5 basis points of positive impact.”

  • “Noninterest expenses were up $1.4 million linked quarter to $186 million,” and “salaries and benefit expenses were down $0.6 million on a linked quarter basis,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants) and (Executive VP & CFO Derrick Walsh), while Walsh added “professional services fees were up $1.6 million” and “FDIC and regulatory fees increased $1.6 million quarter-over-quarter.”

  • “Ending deposit balances were $22.4 billion,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants), who also said Axos “deliberately reduced higher cost savings and time deposits and temporarily increased Federal Home Loan Bank advances in anticipation of the roughly $2.3 billion of Jenius Bank deposits coming in the June quarter.”

Q&A

  • Kyle Peterson, Needham & Company, LLC: asked why securities rose and whether it was pre-Jenius liquidity positioning; (Executive VP & CFO Walsh) said Axos bought Treasuries during a market “dislocation” and “that was around $750 million that we moved into those securities,” describing a hedged return pickup versus holding cash at the Fed.

  • Kyle Peterson, Needham & Company, LLC: asked if capital call growth was draws or new clients; (President, CEO & Director Garrabrants) answered, “quite a few new clients,” and added, “I don't expect the cap call side growth will be as big as it was in the next quarter.”

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked if the allowance build was mainly the new C&I nonaccrual; (Executive VP & CFO Walsh) said, “the C&I was the biggest aspect of it,” and (President, CEO & Director Garrabrants) said on the syndicated credit, “we're now the agent, and we're working with the sponsor.”

  • Brooks Dutton, Jefferies: asked about temporary borrowings’ NIM impact and reversal post-Jenius; (Executive VP & CFO Walsh) said it was “maybe a basis point or 2,” and (President, CEO & Director Garrabrants) said, “I don't want to introduce all those clients to the bank with a rate cut.”

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked if deposit acquisitions enable pricing down funding and whether liquidity builds could pressure results; (President, CEO & Director Garrabrants) said, “I wouldn't really model in any significant sort of increase in NIM,” while (Executive VP & CFO Walsh) said potential “gross up” in assets could occur later in calendar 2026, with expectations of “greater than $30 billion of assets.”

  • Liam Coohill, Raymond James: asked about cross-sell to Capital One IRA clients and M&A priorities; (President, CEO & Director Garrabrants) said, “We have no restrictions on our ability to cross-sell securities products to those clients,” and on deal focus, “We're really looking at some of each.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on balance-sheet positioning (securities, borrowings, liquidity), credit migration, and sustainability of growth, including questions like “could you provide any additional color on the type of credit and timing of resolution.”

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, using caution on optimization and near-term growth pacing, including “I wouldn't really model in any significant sort of increase in NIM” and “I don't expect the cap call side growth will be as big.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s more celebratory framing (“outstanding quarter”), the current call placed greater emphasis on deposit acquisitions, diminishing FDIC accretion, and a specific C&I reserve, while continuing to reiterate low-to-mid-teens loan growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter highlighted two deposit acquisitions ($2.3B from Jenius Bank and $3.2B from Capital One) with stated timelines, while the prior quarter focused on the Verdant integration benefits and FDIC accretion outlook.

  • Net interest margin moved from 4.94% (prior quarter) to 4.57% (current quarter), with management attributing prior-quarter NIM to an FDIC prepayment benefit and the current-quarter movement to normalization and a smaller remaining FDIC uplift.

  • Credit discussion shifted from improvement in NPAs and very low charge-offs (prior quarter) to addressing a new syndicated C&I nonperformer and a higher provision tied to a specific reserve (current quarter).

Risks and Concerns

  • “One syndicated C&I shared national credit became delinquent this quarter,” said (President, CEO & Director Gregory Garrabrants), adding, “we have taken over as agent in the syndicated loan and are actively working to resolve this nonperforming loan.”

  • “Provision for credit losses was $41 million…[with] a specific reserve of approximately $20 million for C&I loan,” said (Executive VP & CFO Derrick Walsh), while management also cited model sensitivity to “broader economic events or the geopolitical events.”

  • On margin and funding, (President, CEO & Director Garrabrants) said the company does not plan to reprice Jenius immediately, stating, “we don't really intend to try to optimize a few basis points here or there on NIM.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q3 fiscal 2026 as another quarter of growth in loans, earnings, and fee income, while highlighting a step-down in reported NIM tied to normalization of FDIC-loan accretion and positioning for large deposit acquisitions. Executives emphasized that the $2.3B Jenius deposit conversion is expected to complete next month and that a $3.2B Capital One IRA deposit acquisition is being targeted for the second half of calendar 2026, framing both as liquidity to support “low to mid-teens” annual loan growth. In Q&A, analysts concentrated on liquidity mix shifts, the size and timing of deposit inflows, and a new syndicated C&I credit issue that drove a higher provision and a specific reserve.

data.title: Syndax outlines 2026 operating expense target of approximately $400M as Revuforj expands in NPM1
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Syndax Pharmaceuticals (SNDX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “In the first quarter, we delivered over $100 million in combined sales of Revuforj and Niktimvo, underscoring robust demand for both medicines and advancing the company towards profitability,” said CEO Michael Metzger.
  • “Revuforj net revenue totaled $49 million in the first quarter,” Metzger said, adding that “recent analysis indicates that Revuforj is enabling nearly half of KMT2A patients to receive a potentially curative stem cell transplant, a significant increase from our prior estimates of 33%.”
  • “Niktimvo delivered $55 million in net revenue in the first quarter,” Metzger said, noting it reflected “consistent new patient starts, partially offset by natural attrition among the large cohort of predominantly later-line patients.”
  • “We are well positioned to be the first to get a menin inhibitor approved in frontline AML with strong global site activation and patient enrollment underway in our pivotal trials,” Metzger said.
  • “Total revenue for the first quarter was $64.9 million,” CFO Keith Goldan said, and “we are well funded to continue investing in our commercial and development priorities with $352.1 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities as of March 31st, 2026.”

Outlook

  • “We will continue to expand the body of evidence supporting our medicines with multiple important data readouts anticipated in the second quarter and throughout the year,” CEO Michael Metzger said, highlighting “new maintenance data, which will be highlighted in an oral session at ASCO.”
  • “We are also nearing 2 potentially transformative Niktimvo readouts in the fourth quarter, including Phase II data in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, or IPF, and in newly diagnosed chronic GVHD patients treated with Niktimvo plus Jakafi,” Metzger said.
  • “We are now expecting top line data from the Phase II trial of axatilimab plus ruxolitinib in the fourth quarter, a time line which pulled in due to faster-than-anticipated accrual,” said Head of R&D and CMO Nicholas Botwood.
  • “Guidance remains at total R&D plus SG&A expenses in 2026 of approximately $400 million, excluding the impact of $50 million in estimated non-cash stock compensation expense,” CFO Keith Goldan said.
  • “Sorry, we don't provide revenue guidance,” Goldan said.

Financial results

  • Total revenue was $64.9 million vs. $69,825,520.0 (analysts’ estimate) for fiscal Q1 2026; EPS was not disclosed in the transcript.
  • “Demand for Revuforj remains strong with $48.9 million in net revenue,” CFO Keith Goldan said, adding that results came “even with typical first quarter dynamics, including severe winter storms” and “a growing number of KMT2A patients temporarily pausing Revuforj to proceed to transplant.”
  • “Our 50% share of the net commercial profit totaling $15.9 million in collaboration revenue in the first quarter,” Goldan said, describing the Niktimvo contribution as “within the range of 25% to 30% that we've been guiding to.”
  • “Inventory levels remain within the 2- to 3-week range we have previously guided,” Goldan said.

Q&A

  • Anupam Rama, JPMorgan: asked what to watch for in upcoming multicenter real-world Revuforj data and whether it includes KMT2A and NPM1; Head of R&D and CMO Botwood said, “I'm not going to talk about the data specifically,” but flagged “broad coverage across the genetic subtypes, including NUP98,” plus focus areas including “the number of patients that are progressing to transplant” and “the ability to get patients on to maintenance.”
  • Corinne Jenkins, Goldman Sachs: asked to quantify the transplant headwind and update KMT2A penetration; CEO Michael Metzger said, “we do think we'll get nicely north of 50% this year,” while CCO Steven Closter said restart timing “is probably 3 to 4 months” on average, with some patients “6 months or more off treatment.”
  • Philip Nadeau, TD Cowen: asked what gates NPM1 penetration and whether FLT3 combo data matters; CCO Closter said, “it's a different market,” and “the time to peak will just be a little bit longer in the NPM1 population relative to KMT2A.”
  • Bradley Canino, Guggenheim: asked when transplant/maintenance flips from headwind to tailwind and about gross-to-net; CCO Closter said, “I don't expect it to be much higher than 50%,” and CFO Keith Goldan said, “we expect gross to nets to settle in the 20% to 25% range... and I'm not changing our guidance at this time.”
  • Josh (for Steve Willey), Stifel: asked whether axatilimab+rux Phase II could support compendia use; Botwood said, “if you see a meaningful improvement... I think that could not only inform clinical practice, but potentially also compendia listing.”
  • Barclays: asked for 2026 revenue guidance and competitive dynamics; Goldan said, “we don't provide revenue guidance,” and Closter said, “we think it's a good thing that there's another menin player in the market, raising awareness.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing on near-term revenue mechanics, with repeated challenges on the transplant “headwind,” restart timing, and access frictions (e.g., step edits), alongside requests for revenue guidance.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing timing and evidence-building; CEO Michael Metzger said, “we do expect in future quarters to start to see some of the compounding effect,” while also acknowledging restart timing variability: “it's more like 5, 6 months, maybe even longer.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, the tone shifted toward defending/clarifying commercial mechanics (transplant pause, restarts, gross-to-net seasonality) rather than celebrating launch momentum, while maintaining confidence in leadership positioning.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Revuforj net revenue increased to $49 million from $44.2 million, while Niktimvo net revenue was $55 million vs. $56 million in Q4.
  • The transplant narrative sharpened: management moved from “approximately 1/3 of KMT2A patients” proceeding to transplant in Q4 to “nearly half” in Q1, with greater focus on the short-term pause vs. longer-term maintenance opportunity.
  • Maintenance restarts were discussed as still building, with Q4 citing “approximately 40% to 45%” restarted and Q1 reiterating “around 45%” alongside comments that some patients can take “5, 6 months, maybe even longer” to return.
  • Cash and funding language stayed consistent (profitability path with stable expenses), with cash declining to $352.1 million from $394 million at the prior quarter-end.

Risks and concerns

  • “Severe winter storms... had a transient impact on both products,” CFO Keith Goldan said.
  • Management repeatedly flagged the KMT2A transplant pause as a near-term commercial headwind, with CEO Michael Metzger stating, “the robust transplant rate seen with Revuforj has a short-term impact on the business.”
  • Competitive and access risk remained a topic in Q&A; CCO Steven Closter said step edits existed for “literally 3 plans,” and “it's less than 1% of the overall.”
  • Seasonality and pricing dynamics were noted around gross-to-net, with Goldan citing “typical 1Q seasonality impacts... due to the high deductible commercial resets and the Part D donut hole.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a step toward profitability driven by over $100 million in combined quarterly product sales, with Revuforj’s NPM1 expansion and a higher KMT2A transplant rate reshaping near-term revenue cadence while potentially extending longer-term duration via post-transplant maintenance. Leadership emphasized multiple 2026 catalysts—ASCO/EHA/ASH data flow, frontline menin inhibitor trials, and fourth-quarter readouts in IPF and frontline chronic GVHD—while holding 2026 operating expense guidance at approximately $400 million and reiterating that the company does not provide revenue guidance.

data.title: Sandisk forecasts Q4 revenue of $7,750M-$8,250M while authorizing a $6B share buyback
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sandisk (SNDK) Q3 FY2026

Management View

  • "We delivered another strong quarter with excellent performance across all key metrics" and "have successfully advanced those conversations with 5 multiyear partnerships signed so far" (Chairman & CEO David V. Goeckeler). He said the agreements are "structured to lock in committed supply for our customers and committed financials for Sandisk" and that "our customers' commitments are backed by firm financial guarantees."

  • "Data center is a clear example of this strategy in action with revenue growing 233% sequentially" (Chairman & CEO David V. Goeckeler). He tied data center demand to AI-driven infrastructure changes, adding, "we expect to begin shipping our QLC Stargate solutions for revenue" in fiscal Q4, and said Sandisk’s Q3 revenue was "enhanced by strong demand for our TLC-based enterprise SSD portfolio."

  • "We signed 3 agreements in the third quarter and an additional 2 so far in the fourth quarter" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He added, "the 3 contracts signed during the quarter provide minimum contractual revenue of approximately $42 billion" and said the five agreements "include financial guarantees that exceed $11 billion" with "$0.4 billion in prepayments" included in Q3 balance sheet.

  • "Today, we're announcing that our Board of Directors have authorized a $6 billion share buyback program" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He said the authorization is "effective immediately with no expiration date" and framed it within priorities to "invest in the business, achieve a net cash position and then return cash to shareholders."

Outlook

  • "For the fourth quarter, we forecast revenue between $7,750 million to $8,250 million" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He guided non-GAAP gross margin to "between 79% and 81%" and non-GAAP EPS to "between $30 and $33" (assuming "158 million fully diluted shares").

  • "We expect non-GAAP operating expenses between $480 million and $500 million" and "non-GAAP tax expenses between $775 million and $875 million" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He also guided "non-GAAP interest and other income between $10 million and $30 million."

  • Compared with last quarter’s guidance for Q3, management’s Q4 ranges imply a higher revenue and EPS outlook than the prior quarter’s guided levels, while gross margin guidance remains at elevated levels (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso).

Financial Results

  • "Revenue for the third quarter was $5,950 million, up 97% sequentially and up 251% year-over-year" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He said it "was driven by both a mix shift towards higher-value customers and higher pricing" and noted bit shipments were "down high teens sequentially" as the company built inventory for "strong BiCS8 QLC demand" and for signed agreements.

  • "Sequentially, data center revenue grew 233% to $1,467 million. Edge grew 118% to $3,663 million, and consumer came in at $820 million, down 10%" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso).

  • "Our non-GAAP gross margin for the third quarter was 78.4%" and "Non-GAAP EPS was $23.41" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He cited reconciliation items including "$20 million in stock-based compensation" and "$46 million related to the write-off of unamortized issuance fees" tied to repayment of the remaining "$650 million" term loan balance.

  • "We generated $2,955 million in adjusted free cash flow" and "Cash flow from operations came in at $3,038 million" (Executive VP & CFO Luis Visoso). He reported "Gross capital expenditures totaled $240 million" and the quarter ended with "$3,735 million in cash and cash equivalents."

Q&A

  • Mark Newman, Bernstein: "the EPS guidance you've given $30 to $33...does imply that the rate of price increase is slowing" and asked about conservatism and how price is fixed in long-term agreements; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler responded, "it's early in the quarter, and it's an extremely dynamic market...but we're very confident in the numbers," while CFO Visoso added, "The shorter time you are in within the contract, the more fixed it is...as you go out, there is a little bit more variable."

  • Joseph Moore, Morgan Stanley: asked how much of enterprise SSD growth is market vs. portfolio; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said, "it starts with the portfolio" and "there is a strong market pull," adding enterprise SSDs "was 25% of the portfolio this quarter, and we expect that to increase."

  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius Research: asked if contracted bits could go above 50% and about margin targets; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said, "I definitely think it can get above 50%" and on margins, "I don't think we're there yet to talk about that...we're very, very focused on getting the cyclicality out of this business."

  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about NAND supply/demand balance and buybacks; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said Sandisk can drive mid- to high-teens bit growth "through nodal transitions" and called NAND a "spectacular cash generator" as CapEx as a percent of revenue declines; CFO Visoso responded on capital return, "we just announced a $6 billion buyback" and "we'll keep on tracking our cash flow."

  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked if any top U.S. hyperscalers are in contracts and whether Sandisk will disclose contract metrics, plus Kioxia supply discussions; CFO Visoso said, "We're not going to disclose the names of our customers" and "we will provide you this RPO metric" quarterly; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said on supply growth, "No. We still have the same plans" with Kioxia.

  • Jake (for Aaron Rakers), Wells Fargo: asked about Stargate ramp and KV cache/agentic AI; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said Stargate has been "under qualification...for well over a year" and on KV cache-related demand, "it's an extremely, extremely dynamic situation" and emphasized customers are putting up "billions of dollars of collateral" in agreements.

  • Asiya Merchant, Citigroup: asked about PCs/phones snapping back and supply confidence, plus CapEx trajectory; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said units are "down" now but he expects them "flattening out up slightly in '27" and added, "we're going to supply the customers that we have agreements with" while discussing extending NBMs to edge; CFO Visoso said CapEx should be modeled "more in dollars" with "a little bit more higher CapEx" over the next several quarters for more expensive conversions.

  • Vijay Rakesh, Mizuho: asked whether RPO/guarantees are mostly data center and asked about high-bandwidth flash; CFO Visoso said Sandisk is "not disclosing the customers" and explained the $42 billion figure is the "minimum contractual revenue" for the three Q3-signed deals; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said on high-bandwidth flash, "We're happy with how it's going" and reiterated a timeline of "having actual the NAND late this year" and "more of the system with the controller early mid next year."

  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA: asked about pricing restrictions in contracts and TLC/QLC mix; CFO Visoso said, "We can't go into pricing in detail" beyond "fixed price components and some variable"; Chairman & CEO Goeckeler said portfolio is "roughly 2/3 TLC, 1/3 QLC" and that data center is "predominantly TLC" ahead of the QLC launch.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, frequently highlighting results (e.g., "phenomenal set of results" and "great results") but pressed on sustainability and structure, repeatedly probing contract pricing, margin durability, and data center mix (multiple analysts).

  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks (e.g., "We delivered another strong quarter" and "We believe our margins are sustainable") and became more emphatic in Q&A around contract enforceability (Chairman & CEO Goeckeler: "nothing can be further from the truth" regarding contract "teeth").

  • Versus the prior quarter, management moved from describing NBMs as being "engaged in discussions" to asserting execution with "5 multiyear partnerships signed," while analyst focus shifted from whether LTAs would be signed to how pricing, RPO disclosure, and margins work under those agreements.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call emphasized execution and scale of NBMs—"5 multiyear partnerships signed" and "$42 billion" of minimum contractual revenue for three deals—whereas the prior call highlighted early progress, including "one agreement" signed and "several in the queue" (Executive VP & CFO Visoso).

  • Data center prominence increased: current quarter reported "revenue growing 233% sequentially" in data center (Chairman & CEO Goeckeler), compared with prior quarter’s reported 64% sequential data center growth and continued qualification updates.

  • Capital allocation messaging advanced from prioritizing debt reduction and building cash reserves to announcing "a $6 billion share buyback program" after stating the company achieved a net cash position (Executive VP & CFO Visoso).

Risks and Concerns

  • "It's early in the quarter, and it's an extremely dynamic market" and management indicated conservatism around near-term pricing assumptions (Chairman & CEO Goeckeler).

  • Management flagged variability across NBMs: "These agreements are really tailored to individual customers" with pricing that includes "fixed elements" and "variable elements" (Chairman & CEO Goeckeler; Executive VP & CFO Visoso).

  • Edge and client market uncertainty persisted alongside expectations of improvement: PC and phone units are "down" with a view they are "flattening out" and improving in fiscal 2027 (Chairman & CEO Goeckeler).

Final Takeaway

Sandisk’s management framed fiscal Q3 as a step-change quarter driven by higher pricing, mix shift, and rapid data center expansion, while positioning signed multiyear supply partnerships—supported by stated minimum contractual revenue and financial guarantees—as the core mechanism to increase demand visibility and reduce cyclicality. For fiscal Q4, management guided to higher revenue, elevated gross margin, and higher EPS, and paired that outlook with a newly authorized $6 billion share buyback, while reiterating that contract structures vary by customer and that the market remains highly dynamic early in the quarter.
